If you're not safe right now

Please reach out right now.

You don't have to manage this alone. Help is available 24/7.

🚨 Immediate danger — call 911

If you or someone else is at risk of harm right now, call 911.

988 — Call or Text

Free, 24/7, confidential. For mental health and substance use crises. A real person answers.

Crisis Text Line

Text HOME to 741741 if talking feels like too much right now.

Hope for Wellness — Indigenous peoples

Call 1-855-242-3310 (24/7). English, French, Cree, Ojibway, Inuktitut.

Trans Lifeline

Call 1-877-330-6366 — peer support run by trans people, for trans people.

While you wait: press your feet into the floor. Take one slow breath in, then out. Look around and name three things you can see. You're here. Help is coming.

Having a hard time → Low or shut down

Sometimes the hardest thing isn't too much. It's nothing at all.

What's happening

When the nervous system gets overwhelmed — by stress, loss, chronic pressure, or simply too much for too long — it sometimes does the opposite of panic. It goes quiet. Flat. Numb. Shut down.

This isn't laziness. It isn't weakness. It's a protective response — your system doing what it learned to do when things got to be too much.

It won't stay like this.

The waiting trap

Most people wait to feel motivated before they act. That's backwards.

Motivation doesn't come first. Action does. Even small, pointless action — standing up, making something warm to drink, stepping outside for sixty seconds — creates the neurological conditions for motivation to follow.

You don't have to want to do it. You just have to do something small enough that it doesn't require wanting.

Behavioural activation is one of the most evidence-supported approaches for low mood and depression. The idea is simple: when we're low, we withdraw — and withdrawal deepens the low. Small actions, even ones that feel pointless, interrupt that cycle. They don't have to be meaningful. They don't have to feel good. They just have to happen. Over time, the nervous system starts to associate movement with something other than flatness.

One small move — right now

Not a task. Not a plan. Just one thing that involves your body in the world:

  • Stand up and walk to a different room
  • Open a window and look outside for thirty seconds
  • Make something warm to drink
  • Put on one song — anything
  • Step outside, even just to the doorstep

You don't have to feel better after. You just have to move.

What's one small thing you could do right now?

Even something that feels pointless. Even getting up counts.

If you want to understand what you're feeling

Numbness often has something underneath it. You don't have to find it right now — but if you want to look:

If this has been going on for a while

Persistent low mood, shutdown, and flatness that doesn't lift can be signs that something more is going on. That's worth talking to someone about.

Find a therapist Psychology Today Canada ConnectOntario — 1-866-531-2600 24/7 navigation to mental health services

What would feel most useful right now?

Press your feet into the floor. One slow breath. You're here now.

Having a hard time → Start here

You don't have to know what's wrong.

You just have to be here.

No pressure to choose the right thing. Start wherever feels easiest.

Having a hard time → Where do you feel it most?

Where do you feel it most?

Press your feet into the floor. One slow breath. You're here now.

If you're having thoughts that life isn't worth living, please reach out. Call or text 988 any time.

Getting curious → Grief

Grief doesn't ask you to let go. It asks you to make room.

Grief doesn't always look the way we expect. Sometimes it's confusing, quiet, or completely misunderstood by the people around you. See if any of these feel familiar.

Ambivalent grief

Missing someone who also hurt you. Grieving a relationship that needed to end. Feeling the loss of something that was also harmful.

This is one of the most disorienting forms of grief — because two true things are in conflict at the same time.

A few things worth knowing:

  • Love and harm aren't mutually exclusive. You can love someone who hurt you. The love was real. That doesn't make the harm less real either.
  • Grief follows attachment, not worthiness. The brain doesn't only attach to safe people. Missing someone harmful doesn't mean you're endorsing what they did.
  • There's a layered loss. You're not only grieving the person — you're also grieving the version of them you needed them to be, and the relationship you deserved to have.

If your mind is saying "I should be over this" or "something is wrong with me for still missing them" — that thought is the grief, not the evidence.

You're allowed to miss them and know it was right to go.

Disenfranchised grief

A loss others don't recognise or validate. A friendship that ended. A miscarriage. A pet. A version of yourself you had to leave behind. A future you had planned and will now never have.

These losses are real. The absence of a funeral doesn't make them smaller.

Anticipatory grief

Grieving something before it's gone. A diagnosis. A relationship ending slowly. Watching someone you love decline. You're already feeling the loss before it arrives.

That's not weakness. That's love running ahead of time.

Complicated grief

When grief doesn't move. When it stays as acute as the day it arrived, months or years later. When it interrupts daily life and won't settle.

This one usually needs more support than any tool can offer — but naming it is the first step.

The jar

Grief is like a ball inside a jar. At first, the jar is full — there's little room for anything else. Over time, the grief doesn't necessarily shrink. But the jar can expand — as new experiences, relationships, roles, and moments of joy are added. Life and grief learn to exist side by side.

The goal isn't to make the ball smaller. It's to keep building the jar.

This metaphor is adapted from the work of Dr. Lois Tonkin, a New Zealand grief researcher and counsellor, who developed it to help people understand that healing from grief isn't about the pain disappearing — it's about life growing around it.

Is there anything that has expanded in your jar, even slightly — alongside the grief?

Even something small counts. You don't have to have an answer.

Right now

You don't have to do anything else with this. But if it would help — grief lives in the body first:

If grief isn't moving

Some grief needs more than a tool can hold. That's not a failure — it's information. Speaking with someone trained in grief can make a real difference.

Find a grief-informed therapist Search "bereavement" or "grief" — Psychology Today Canada ConnectOntario — 1-866-531-2600 24/7 navigation to mental health services in Ontario 988 — Call or text If you're in crisis

Having a hard time → Understand what's happening

What's pulling at you?

Press your feet into the floor. One slow breath. You're here now.

Understand myself → What to explore?

That's a good place to be.

What would you like to explore?

Something coming → Set a boundary or communicate something

The goal isn't to win. It's to say what's true in a way that maintains our relationships.

These skills work best in close personal relationships — friendships, partnerships, and family.

DEARMAN — Ask for what you need, or say no

Use this when you need to make a request or hold a boundary clearly. Fill in each step with your situation — then see your script.

D — Describe the situation, just the facts

E — Express how you feel. Use "I feel" not "you made me"

A — Assert what you need, clearly and directly

R — Reinforce — what's in it for the relationship?

M — stay Mindful. Stay on your point. If they push back, deflect or redirect — don't get pulled into a different argument.
A — appear Willing. Show you're open to a middle ground, even if you're firm on the core need.
N — Negotiate. Ask what might work for them. "What would feel fair to you?"

Your script

GIVE — Stay connected while you say it

Use this to keep the relationship warm while the conversation is hard.

G — be Gentle. What's one thing you want to avoid saying or doing?

I — act Interested. What's one question you could ask to show you're listening?

V — Validate. How could you acknowledge their perspective, even if you disagree?

E — Easy manner. Keep your tone light where you can. A little warmth goes a long way — even in a hard conversation.

Your approach

FAST — Keep your self-respect intact

Use this to stay grounded in who you are, regardless of how the conversation goes.

F — be Fair. What would a fair outcome look like for both of you?

A — no Apologies. Don't apologise for having a need or a boundary. You can apologise for how you say something — not for what you need.

S — Stick to your values. What value are you protecting in this conversation?

T — be Truthful. What's the one true thing you most need to say?

Your anchor

Keep preparing:

Something coming → Prepare

Take one slow breath.

Notice what's already in your body — tension, anticipation, dread.

You don't have to resolve it. Just notice it's there.

What would help most before you go in?

Using tools before you need them — that's how they become reliable when you do.

Too much → Grounding

Feet on the floor.

Look around. Name 3 things you can see.

Stay here for 10 seconds.

Nothing to figure out right now.

Start here:

5-4-3-2-1 Grounding

Bring your attention back through your senses.

  • 5 things you can see
  • 4 things you can touch — feel the texture
  • 3 sounds you can hear
  • 2 things you can smell
  • 1 thing you can taste
Your senses only exist right now — in this moment. Moving attention through them pulls you out of rumination and back into the present. You don't have to fight the overwhelm. You just redirect your focus, and the intensity drops.

Or try something else:

Paced Breathing

Inhale for 4 counts, exhale slowly for 6 counts. Repeat 2–3 minutes. The longer exhale is what calms your nervous system.

Ready
Making your exhale longer than your inhale sends a calming signal through your nervous system. Even 2–3 slow breaths can measurably lower your heart rate. It's always with you.

The 6/10 Rule

If your distress is above a 6/10, ground first. You don't need to process everything at full intensity — work in smaller, safer amounts. Presence, not overwhelm, is the goal.

When emotional intensity gets very high, the thinking brain starts to disengage — which means trying to process or "work through" a feeling at full intensity often makes it worse, not better. Working with difficult emotions in smaller doses teaches your nervous system that feelings are survivable and temporary. Over time this builds genuine tolerance — not suppression, but the ability to stay present with difficult states without needing to escape them. The goal isn't to avoid feeling. It's to feel in amounts you can actually metabolise.
"You don't have to fix it all right now. You just have to get through this moment."

Even a small shift counts.

Did this help even a little?

You stayed with it instead of running from it.

That's not small.

Grounding is worth coming back to.

If this helped, you don't have to hold it alone.

That's okay. Stay with me. Try this instead.

It sounds like this might be too much for a tool right now. Reaching out is the right move.

Need to say something hard to get there?

I'm overwhelmed → Body tools

Feet on the floor.

Look around. Name 3 things you can see.

Stay here for 10 seconds.

Just focus on this.

Start here:

Temperature Shift

Run cold water over your wrists or splash your face. This activates your body's dive reflex — it immediately slows your heart rate and lowers arousal. Fast, simple, no equipment needed.

⚠️ If you have heart, blood pressure, or circulation concerns, check with your care team first.

Cold water on the face triggers the diving reflex — an automatic physiological response that slows your heart rate and shifts blood flow within seconds. It doesn't require thought or effort. It's one of the few techniques that works on the nervous system directly rather than through the mind. Even cold water on your wrists activates a related pathway through sudden sensory contrast.

Or try something else:

Physiological Sigh

Double inhale through the nose (breathe in, then sniff a little more), then one long slow exhale through your mouth. Repeat 2–3 times. Research shows even one round noticeably lowers stress.

When stressed, your lungs don't fully expand. The double inhale fills them completely. The long exhale tells your nervous system it's safe to relax.

Paired Muscle Relaxation

Tense a muscle group as you inhale — try your fists, shoulders, or jaw. Hold for 5 seconds. Release completely as you exhale. Notice the contrast. Repeat 2–3 times.

Under stress, your muscles hold low-level tension without your awareness. Deliberately tensing a muscle group first makes that tension conscious and then gives it somewhere to go. The contrast between tension and release trains your nervous system to recognize what relaxation actually feels like — which becomes increasingly hard to detect under chronic stress. A few rounds also engages focused attention, which competes with anxious thought loops.

Feet on the Floor

Press your feet firmly into the ground. Move your toes. Say quietly: "I am in [room]. I am okay right now." Repeat until you feel more anchored.

Feeling the weight of your body sends a direct signal to your nervous system that you are here and safe. Especially helpful when you feel checked out or disconnected.

Even a small shift counts.

Did this help even a little?

You worked with your body instead of fighting it.

Your body knows how to settle. That's always available.

If this helped, you don't have to hold it alone.

That's okay. Stay with me. Try this instead.

It sounds like you might need more than a tool right now.

Too much → Name what's here

Put a hand on your chest or stomach.

Notice what's there. Don't name it yet.

Just feel the physical texture of it — weight, tightness, heat, emptiness.

What's the overall texture of what you're feeling?

Pick what fits — you can choose more than one.

Now look more closely.

Select everything that fits — even if it surprises you.

Shame is worth naming carefully. It often hides underneath other feelings.

This is what's here right now:

That took courage to name.

Notice if anything shifts just from seeing it named.

Naming is the first move.

Too much → Emotions

Hand on your chest. Feel it rise and fall.

That's enough for right now.

Whatever is there — it's allowed to be there.

Working with heavy feelings

Name It to Tame It

Labelling an emotion — "I'm angry," "I feel grief" — actually reduces its intensity in the brain. The more specific, the better. "I feel embarrassed" calms more than "I feel bad."

Where do you feel it in your body? Tight chest → anxiety or anger. Heavy chest → grief. Heat in face → anger or shame. Stomach drop → fear.

Brain imaging research shows that putting a word to a feeling activates the prefrontal cortex — the thinking brain — while reducing activity in the amygdala, which drives emotional reactivity. This happens even with a single word. The more specific the label, the stronger the effect — "I feel embarrassed" produces more settling than "I feel bad." You're not dismissing the feeling. You're changing its signal strength.

Guided

Don't know what you're feeling? Name it precisely — takes 3 minutes.

Guilt vs Shame — An Important Difference

Guilt says: "I did something I don't feel good about." It's about behaviour — and can guide repair.
Shame says: "I am bad, broken, unworthy." It's about identity — and shuts people down.

Which one are you carrying right now?

Guilt can motivate repair and change. Shame tends to create hiding, avoidance, and escape — and increases risk. Self-compassion makes accountability feel safe enough to stay with.

Emotions Come in Waves

Even strong feelings usually peak and pass within minutes when not fought or avoided. Staying present without escaping — that's how emotions move through instead of getting stuck.

"I can stay with this feeling. It will rise and fall."

Emotions are temporary — they have a beginning, a peak, and an end. What stretches them out is what happens when they arrive. Fighting an emotion — trying to suppress it, reason it away, or escape it — keeps the emotional loop active. Avoiding it delays its completion. When you stay present with a feeling without trying to change it, you allow it to move through its natural arc. The peak itself is usually minutes. It's the story we tell afterward — and the effort to push it away — that makes it feel permanent.

What's Underneath the Anger

Anger often protects something more vulnerable. Ask: "If I weren't angry right now, what would I be feeling?" Hurt, fear, and shame are common answers. That's usually where the real need lives.

Anger can be a primary emotion — but often it's secondary, covering something that feels more exposing: hurt, fear, grief, shame. Acting from the surface emotion tends to miss the actual need. Asking "what would I feel if I weren't angry?" creates a moment of honest contact with what's actually driving it. That's where the real work — and the real relief — usually lives.

You don't have to resolve the anger. Just get one layer closer to what's underneath it.

Even a small shift counts.

Did this help even a little?

You moved toward the feeling instead of away from it.

Naming it helps. That's yours to keep.

If this helped, you don't have to hold it alone.

That's okay. Stay with me. Try this instead.

It sounds like this needs more than a tool right now.

Too much → Finding direction

Feeling stuck is information, not failure.

Finding Direction

Control Check

Ask: "Is this something I can actually influence right now?"

  • Yes → Take one small action. Just one.
  • No → Move to acceptance and grounding. Rumination pretends to be problem-solving. It usually isn't.
Research shows rumination increases anxiety while reducing your ability to solve things. The control check interrupts this by asking one question: can I act on this or not? If not, acceptance tools — not more thinking.

One Small Thing

What's the single smallest action you could take today? Not the solution — just the next right step. What's the easiest, most manageable first move?

Action doesn't have to be big to be meaningful. One small thing is still forward motion.

When you're stuck, the brain narrows — options shrink, problems feel monolithic, and anything short of a complete solution feels pointless. This is why large goals fail under distress and small actions don't. A small action doesn't require the problem to be solved first. It just requires one step to be possible right now. Small actions also produce evidence: proof that movement is possible, which directly counters the paralysis that stress creates. Momentum is built from small, repeated steps — not from waiting until you feel ready.

Sort What's Yours to Carry

Some of what's weighing on you might not actually be yours. Which of your current challenges feel personal — rooted in your habits or thoughts? Which are shaped by external things — work demands, housing, systems, or other people's choices?

Just because someone handed it to you doesn't mean it was yours to hold.

People under chronic stress often carry a mix of personal challenges and systemic ones — poverty, discrimination, housing instability, workplace inequity — but experience all of it as personal failure. Sorting what is genuinely yours from what belongs to a system or another person isn't denial — it's accuracy. When you stop fighting something that isn't yours, you free up real energy for the parts that are. The load doesn't disappear, but it becomes the right size.

Guided

Sort what's yours to carry — and what isn't.

If/Then Plan

When you don't know what to do next, commit to one conditional:

"If [this moment], I will [one small action]."

Example: "If I'm still stuck after 10 minutes, I'll text one person."

You're not solving it. You're giving yourself a direction when the moment comes.

If/then plans — implementation intentions — are one of the most reliably effective behaviour change tools in research. Deciding in advance what you'll do in a specific situation removes the need for willpower in the moment. The decision is already made. You just follow through on a plan your calmer self created.

Even a small shift counts.

Did this help even a little?

You looked at the situation instead of just spinning in it.

Sorting what's yours makes the rest more manageable.

If this helped, you don't have to hold it alone.

That's okay. Stay with me. Try this instead.

Some stuck feelings need more than a tool. Reaching out is the right move.

Is there a conversation that needs to happen?

I'm overwhelmed → Alone in this

You don't have to explain yourself for this to be real.

The specific loneliness

There's a particular kind of alone that isn't about being by yourself. It's being surrounded by people who don't quite get it. Carrying something you can't fully explain. Feeling like the weight of it isn't visible to anyone around you.

That's real. And it's more common than people say out loud.

Hyperindependence

"I can only rely on myself" is often a survival strategy, not a preference. If asking for help feels dangerous or shameful, that's worth exploring. Isolation increases risk. Connection protects.

Hyperindependence usually develops in environments where depending on others was genuinely unsafe — where help didn't come, where vulnerability was exploited, or where self-sufficiency was the only reliable option. It's a learned protection, not a personality trait. Recognizing "I don't need anyone" as a survival response, not a preference, creates the possibility of slowly and safely testing whether that's still true.

Say what you need

Pick the sentence that fits and send it — in a text, out loud, or just to yourself to get clear first.

  • "I need some support right now."
  • "I'm not okay and I don't know what I need yet."
  • "Can you just be here with me for a minute?"

You don't need the right words. You just need a direction.

One reach — right now

Not a grand gesture. One text or call to someone safe. "Hey, thinking of you" counts. Connection doesn't have to be a conversation about your struggles to matter.

Having even one supportive person measurably reduces distress. Your brain is wired to share its load with others — problems feel smaller and more manageable when someone else is present. The reach doesn't have to be about your struggle. It just has to be toward someone.

If you have no one to call

ConnectOntario can help you find mental health and social services in your community. Call 1-866-531-2600, 24/7, free, available in multiple languages.

This is a navigation and referral service, not a crisis line or counselling.

Even a small shift counts.

Did this help even a little?

You reached toward connection instead of staying isolated.

Even the smallest reach changes something.

If this helped, you don't have to hold it alone.

That's okay. Stay with me. Try this instead.

Please reach out to someone directly. You don't have to manage this alone.

Thinking → Break the loop

Stop. Don't follow the thought.

Look at where you are right now.

You don't have to figure it out. Just step off the track for a moment.

Guided

Have a specific thought looping? Work through it step by step — takes about 5 minutes.

Tools to break the loop

Control Check

Ask: "Is this something I can actually influence right now?" If yes — take one small action. If no — move to grounding. Rumination pretends to be problem-solving. It usually isn't.

Rumination activates the same brain regions as genuine problem-solving, which is why it feels like you're doing something useful. Research consistently shows the opposite — rumination increases depression and anxiety while actually impairing your ability to solve problems. The control check interrupts this by forcing a single binary question: can I act on this right now, or not? If yes, one small action breaks the loop. If no, you've just removed the justification for continuing to circle it.

Leaves on a Stream

Imagine a gently flowing stream. Each thought that shows up — place it on a leaf and watch it drift away without chasing it. You're the observer, not the thought.

Instead of getting caught up in your thoughts, you practise watching them from a distance. You can't stop your thoughts — and fighting them takes energy. This is easier: notice them, place them on a leaf, let them pass.

Worry Postponement

Write down everything on your mind. Tell yourself: "I'll come back to this at [specific time]." The thoughts don't need to be solved right now. Give your brain permission to set them down.

Most people find that when they return to the worry later, it doesn't feel as urgent or scary.

Trying to stop a worry usually makes it worse — the brain interprets the suppression effort as confirmation that the thought is dangerous, and keeps circling back to it. Postponement works differently: instead of fighting the thought, you acknowledge it and give it a specific time. This sends a signal that the thought has been received and doesn't need to keep repeating. Writing it down adds another layer — externalising the worry onto paper reduces the brain's need to hold it internally. Most people find the urgency drops considerably when they return.

Even a small shift counts.

Did this help even a little?

You interrupted the loop — even briefly. That matters.

Stepping off the track — even briefly — breaks the loop.

If this helped, you don't have to hold it alone.

That's okay. Stay with me. Try this instead.

Loops that won't stop sometimes need more support.

Thinking → Work through a thought

Let's slow this down.

You don't have to follow the thought right now.

It can be there — and you can pause.

Take one slow breath before we continue.

What's the thought?

Write it exactly as it appears in your head.

Don't clean it up — write it the way it actually sounds.

Now try this:

Say it out loud if you can. Or just read it slowly.

Even a small shift here is enough — you don't need to force anything.

See if it feels even slightly different.

That's okay — we can keep going.

Sometimes the mind needs a few passes. Let's continue.

That thought is trying to help in some way.

Usually by warning you about something — even if it gets it wrong.

You don't have to believe it. And you don't have to push it away.

From a bit more distance, what do you want to do with the next few minutes?

You may have created a bit of distance from that thought.

Even a small step back counts.

Did something shift, even slightly?

That distance is a skill. You made it happen.

The thought might still be there — but you're not fully inside it anymore.

Let's try a different angle.

The defusion step is one way in. Here are others.

That's okay.

Sometimes the mind needs a few passes.

You can try again, or take a break and come back.

Thinking → Reframe the inner critic

Something is being said about you right now.

Not by someone else — by a voice in your own head.

Let's look at it together.

What is that voice saying?

Write it as a direct statement — the way it actually sounds.

Don't clean it up.

Read that back:

That sentence — those exact words — your mind produced that.

It's not a fact about you.

It's a voice. And voices can be wrong.

Now imagine someone you care about said this to you — about themselves:

What would you say back to them?

Don't think about what's "right." Just say what you'd actually say.

Or pick what sounds closest to what you'd say:

You just said:

You just said something to that person that you haven't been saying to yourself.

That gap — between how you'd treat them and how you're treating yourself — that's worth sitting with.

You don't have to close it today. But now you can see it.

That makes sense.

Self-compassion is often harder to receive than to give.

Sometimes the critic is there for a reason — it learned that being hard on you was safer than being soft.

You don't have to believe the kinder voice yet. Just notice it exists alongside the other one.

You just moved from one voice to two.

That's not a small thing.

Did something shift, even slightly?

That shift — from critic to witness — is something you just did.

The critic voice will come back. But now you know there's another one.

Where to go from here

That's okay.

Sometimes the mind needs a few passes.

You can try again, or give it some space and come back.

Thinking → Self-talk

The way you talk to yourself matters — especially when things are hard.

Managing Self-Talk

"I'm Having the Thought That…"

Instead of "I'm a failure," try "I'm having the thought that I'm a failure." This creates space between you and the thought — reducing its authority without dismissing it.

Adding "I'm having the thought that..." makes the thought something you're observing — a mental event, not reality. Research shows this reduces emotional intensity without needing to argue with the thought.

The Friend Test

Take the thought. Would you say that exact thing to a close friend who was struggling? If not — it's not fair to say it to yourself either. What would the kinder, more accurate version be?

Most people apply significantly harsher standards to themselves than to anyone they care about — but the double standard is usually not conscious. There's often a genuine belief that "my situation is different" or "I deserve the criticism." The friend test creates a moment of honest comparison that's hard to dismiss. It doesn't tell you what to think — it just highlights the inconsistency, which is often enough to loosen the self-critical loop.

Guided

Reframe the inner critic — step by step.

Thought Diffusion — Lighten the Grip

Try one of these to loosen a sticky thought:

  • Say it out loud in a cartoon voice
  • Sing it to the tune of "Happy Birthday"
  • Write it down and read it slowly — like a script, not a verdict

You don't have to delete the thought. Just loosen its grip.

Thoughts feel authoritative partly because of how seriously we hold them. Singing a thought to a cartoon tune or saying it in a silly voice doesn't change its content — it changes its relationship to you. Instead of the thought feeling like a fact about reality, it becomes something your mind produced. This technique comes from ACT — cognitive defusion — and research shows it reduces the emotional impact and believability of thoughts without needing to argue with them or replace them with something positive.

Even a small shift counts.

Did this help even a little?

You spoke to yourself differently than the critic wanted.

That shift in how you talked to yourself — notice it.

If this helped, you don't have to hold it alone.

That's okay. Stay with me. Try this instead.

Harsh self-talk that won't stop sometimes needs more support.

Thinking → Patterns

Thinking patterns aren't character flaws — they're mind habits. The brain filling in gaps too confidently.

Common Thinking Traps

11 Patterns Worth Knowing

  • Catastrophizing — jumping to worst-case
  • All-or-nothing — no middle ground
  • Mind reading — assuming what others think
  • Fortune telling — predicting failure
  • Emotional reasoning — "I feel like a failure, so I must be"
  • Should statements — rigid rules that create shame
  • Labelling — "I am a failure" vs "I failed at this"
  • Personalization — taking blame for things outside your control
  • Filtering — noticing only the negative
  • Overgeneralising — one event becomes a permanent pattern
  • Magnifying/minimising — inflating the bad, shrinking the good

Which ones feel familiar? You don't have to change them — just naming the pattern interrupts the automatic assumption that the thought is simply true.

Cognitive distortions are systematic — they don't skew randomly, they reliably skew toward threat, failure, or worthlessness. That's why they feel so convincing. When you name the pattern — "that's catastrophizing" — you interrupt the automatic acceptance that what you're thinking is simply true. The thought doesn't disappear, but it loses its authority. That gap between "thought" and "fact" is where change becomes possible.

Thought Record — 4 Steps

  1. Write the thought exactly as it came
  2. Name the pattern (from the list above)
  3. Rewrite it in a fair, honest way — as you'd say to a friend
  4. Notice: did the feeling shift even slightly?
The thought record doesn't work by replacing negative thoughts with positive ones — your brain rejects implausible substitutions. It works by building cognitive flexibility: the ability to generate more than one interpretation of the same event. Under stress, the brain fixes on one reading and treats it as fact. The thought record practises generating alternatives. Even a small shift in certainty — from "this is definitely true" to "this might be true" — changes how much power the thought has.

Try it now

Even a small loosening — from "this is definitely true" to "this might be true" — is the mechanism working.

Even a small shift counts.

Did this help even a little?

You named the pattern — that's the hardest part.

Naming the pattern loosens its grip. Remember that.

If this helped, you don't have to hold it alone.

That's okay. Stay with me. Try this instead.

Persistent distorted thinking sometimes needs professional support.

Thinking → Wise Mind

Wise Mind is where your feelings and your thinking work together — the calm answer, not the loud one.

Accessing Wise Mind

Three Mind States

Emotion Mind — feelings drive decisions. Fast, reactive, intense.
Rational Mind — logic leads, disconnected from what matters.
Wise Mind — the balance point. You notice feelings and think clearly. It often feels like: "This is the next right step."

Most people assume that the more rational they are, the better their decisions. Research says otherwise. Emotion Mind alone is reactive and short-sighted. Reasonable Mind alone strips out the values and relationships that make a decision actually right for you. Wise Mind integrates both — you feel what you feel and you also think clearly. The goal isn't to eliminate emotion from decision-making. It's to stop either mind from running the show alone.

How to Access It Right Now

  • Put both feet flat on the floor
  • Take one slow breath in and out — feel your chest move
  • Ask: "What feels true right now?"
  • Listen for the calm answer, not the loud one

Wise Mind tends to be calm and certain. Emotion Mind is urgent. The quiet, steady answer is usually Wise Mind.

You can't access Wise Mind when your body is flooded — the physical grounding steps come first for a reason. Feet on the floor, one slow breath, feeling your chest move — each of these shifts your body's stress response enough to let the thinking brain re-engage. The question "what feels true right now?" accesses a quieter kind of knowing than direct logical analysis. Wise Mind tends to speak at the same volume as everything else — it's just not urgent. The grounding creates the conditions to hear it.

Try it now.

Feet flat. One breath. Then:

What feels true right now?

Not the loud answer. Not the logical one. The quiet one.

That's it.

That quiet answer — that's the one worth following.

Even a small shift counts.

Did this help even a little?

You listened for the quiet answer instead of the loud one.

The quiet answer is always there when you slow down enough.

If this helped, you don't have to hold it alone.

That's okay. Try settling your body first, then return here.

Sometimes we need more than a tool to access clarity.

Understand myself → Values

Values don't disappear when things get hard. They point forward even when everything else feels unclear.

The Values Vault

What Are Values?

Values are directions, not destinations. You don't complete them — you live toward them. They're not what you should want. They're what actually matters to you, in your bones.

Examples: honesty, connection, freedom, stability, creativity, family, growth, contribution, health, justice, belonging.

Goals can be missed, abandoned, or completed. Values don't go away. That's why they're more reliable as a compass — especially when motivation drops, when circumstances change, or when external pressure makes it hard to know what you actually want. Acting in the direction of a genuine value produces more sustained engagement than acting out of fear or obligation. The key word is genuine — values that come from "what I should care about" don't hold the same weight as ones that come from "what matters to me in my bones."

Finding Yours — Three Questions

  1. When have you felt most alive or most yourself?
  2. What makes you angry about the world? (Often a clue to what you care about.)
  3. If you could only keep three things — relationships, work, freedoms, aspects of yourself — what would they be?
Anger points at violation — something that matters to you is being threatened, ignored, or blocked. That's why it's a reliable clue to values. If injustice makes you furious, justice matters to you. If people being dismissed makes you angry, dignity matters to you. This doesn't mean acting on the anger — it means using it as information about what's important. The same logic applies to grief: what you grieve losing tells you what you valued having.

Values in Action

Choose one value. Ask: "What's the smallest action I could take today that moves me toward this?"

You're not deciding forever — just the next right direction.

When you're overwhelmed, a list of values is just more things to weigh. One value functions as a compass — it reduces the decision to a single question: does this move me toward or away from what matters? Research on values-based action shows that even a brief connection to a genuine value before making a decision improves follow-through and reduces distress. The key word is genuine: a value borrowed from what you think you should care about doesn't carry the same weight as one you actually feel.

If one thing feels clear right now — even partially — you can anchor it here.

If that action involves a conversation with someone:

Even a small shift counts.

Did this help even a little?

You connected with what actually matters — that's the compass.

What matters to you is your compass. It doesn't go away.

If this helped, you don't have to hold it alone.

That's okay. Stay with me. Try this instead.

Feeling disconnected from your values for a long time is worth talking to someone about.

Understand myself → Inner voices

That critical voice in your head isn't the whole story. It's one narrator — with a particular perspective, shaped by specific experiences.

Who's Talking in There?

Name the Voice

Your stress or inner critic isn't you — it's a character passing through. Give it a name: "The Inner Critic," "The Alarm Bell," "The Fence-Sitter," "The Backseat Driver from Hell."

What role does it play? What would you name it?

By externalizing the voice, you create distance between you and the problem — making it easier to manage. The voice becomes something you can observe and question, rather than something you are.

What is that voice saying right now?

Write it as it actually sounds — don't clean it up.

Seeing it written down changes your relationship to it. It becomes something you're looking at — not something you're inside.

Question It

Once you've named the voice, ask:

  • Where did it learn to talk like that?
  • What is it trying to protect you from?
  • What would a more empowering narrator say instead?
Inner critics don't appear from nowhere — they're usually learned from specific environments, relationships, or experiences where that voice served a protective function. Asking "where did this learn to talk like that?" shifts it from being a fact about you to being a response that developed in a context. That's not the same as dismissing it. Often the inner critic was genuinely trying to protect you — from rejection, failure, or danger. Understanding its origin makes it easier to work with rather than against.

That voice is speaking to you.

Not as you — to you.

Can you respond to it, just for a moment — as the one hearing it, not the one saying it?

What would you say back to it?

You just shifted position.

From inside the voice — to in front of it. From here, you can decide whether to follow what it's saying, or take a different direction.

Narrator Swap

Imagine the same thought spoken by a wise friend, a calm therapist, or an older version of yourself who has been through hard things. How would they say it? What would they add?

These alternative narratives aren't less true — they're often more accurate, because they include context and compassion the critic leaves out.

The inner critic presents itself as the objective voice — but it's one narrator with a particular perspective, shaped by specific experiences. It tends to omit context, minimize strengths, and overweight failures. A wise friend or your older self isn't being falsely positive — they're adding information the critic systematically leaves out. Alternative narratives work not because they're more comforting but because they're more complete. That's what makes them clinically meaningful, not just emotionally soothing.

Even a small shift counts.

Did this help even a little?

You looked at the critic directly — most people look away.

Understanding the voice changes your relationship with it.

If this helped, you don't have to hold it alone.

That's okay. Stay with me. Try this instead.

Persistent harsh inner voices sometimes need professional support to address.

Sorting what's yours → Guided

Something is sitting on you.

Before we sort it, let's make it concrete.

What's the situation?

Don't describe it perfectly. Just enough to make it real.

What are you trying to solve, fix, or prevent?

Write it as a list — one thing per line.

Whatever's running. Don't filter it.

Now sort each one.

Be honest. There are no wrong answers.

I control this

I can influence this

Not mine to control

Look at the "not mine" column.

That's weight you've been carrying that doesn't belong to you.

Everything here is yours to act on. That's not more pressure — it's clarity. You know where to put your energy.

The sort is done. Now — what still needs attention?

In what you control — is there one thing you could do today?

Not solve everything. One small thing.

When everything is out of your hands, the question shifts.

Not "what do I do?" but "how do I carry this?"

That's okay — sometimes the sort itself is enough for now.

You've already separated the situation from your reaction to it.

That's further than you were ten minutes ago.

You just put down things that weren't yours to carry.

That's different from ignoring them.

Did something shift, even slightly?

Naming what's not yours doesn't make it disappear.

But it changes your relationship to it. That's what you just did.

Where to go from here

That's okay.

Sometimes just seeing the list clearly is the first step.

You can come back to this when you're ready.

Understand myself → Control

Not everything that lands on your plate is yours to solve.

Guided

Sort what's yours to carry — and what isn't.

Spot What You Can Control

Signs You CAN Influence This

  • You can personally take action that would change or improve it
  • Your thoughts, emotions, or behaviours directly impact the outcome
  • If you change your approach, the situation might improve

Signs This Is OUTSIDE Your Control

  • No matter what you do, the outcome stays the same
  • Other people's decisions, policies, or systems shape the outcome
  • You've tried multiple strategies, and nothing changes

If it's outside your control — accepting this isn't giving up. It's putting your energy where it can actually do something.

Giving up means withdrawing care. Acceptance means stopping the fight against what can't be changed — so you have energy left for what can. Research consistently shows that accepting uncontrollable stressors lowers distress more effectively than continued attempts to change them. This is especially relevant when the stressor is systemic — housing insecurity, discrimination, chronic illness. You're not surrendering to it. You're refusing to let the battle against an unmovable wall exhaust everything you have.

What Can You Actually Do Today?

If you have influence: what's one small, concrete action you could take?
If you don't: what grounding or acceptance tool can help you carry this without fighting it?

Under high stress, the brain narrows its focus — options shrink, the problem feels monolithic, and any action that isn't a complete solution feels pointless. That's why big goals fail under distress and small actions don't. A small action doesn't need the problem to be solved first. It just needs to be doable right now. Over time, small actions compound. They also produce evidence that movement is possible, which counters the paralysis that chronic stress creates.

Even a small shift counts.

Did this help even a little?

You sorted what's yours to carry — that changes how heavy it feels.

Knowing what's yours to carry makes it lighter.

If this helped, you don't have to hold it alone.

That's okay. Stay with me. Try this instead.

Sometimes the load is too much for one person. Reaching out is wise.

Does acting on this involve a conversation?

Understand myself → Identity

Recovery and growth are not about going back to who you were. They're about choosing who you are becoming.

Identity & Reconstruction

Survival Identities vs Chosen Identities

Some identities formed to help you survive — "the strong one who doesn't need anything," "the reliable one who never asks for help." These were proof you became who you needed to be.

Recovery gives you room to choose differently. Not erasing who you were — adding back what got left out.

Survival identities aren't weaknesses — they're adaptive responses to real environments. "The strong one who doesn't need anything" made sense in a situation where showing need was unsafe or ineffective. "The one who holds it together" worked when someone had to. The problem is that these roles are context-specific, but they tend to generalize. In recovery and growth, the old environment may no longer be present — but the identity persists because it worked, and the brain doesn't easily let go of what worked. Recognizing it as a response rather than a fixed truth is the first step to expanding it.

Reconstruction Prompts

  • "One part of me I want to reconnect with is ___."
  • "One part of me that survived despite everything is ___."
  • "The person I'm building toward includes ___."

You are not starting over. You are adding to your story with more intention.

Affirmations — "I am strong, I am worthy" — often fail because they make a claim the brain doesn't yet believe. These prompts work differently. They don't ask you to declare something. They ask you to notice and name specific things that are already true: a part of you that survived, a part you want to reconnect with. That's not a statement about who you are — it's evidence. Evidence accumulates in a way that declarations don't. The brain updates on concrete specifics much more readily than on general positive claims.

Take a moment to actually answer these — even a few words.

"It's never too late to be what you might have been."

Even a small shift counts.

Did this help even a little?

You stayed with uncertainty — that's where rebuilding begins.

The version of you building this — that's real.

If this helped, you don't have to hold it alone.

That's okay. Stay with me. Try this instead.

Deep identity questions sometimes need to be explored with support.

Understand myself → Bigger picture

Not everything hard in your life is a personal failing. Some of it is the system.

It's Not Just You

Seeing the Bigger Picture

Stress doesn't only come from inside us. It also comes from housing insecurity, racism, poverty, ableism, workplace inequity, lack of access to services, and systems that weren't built for everyone.

When you internalize a system's failure as a personal flaw, you carry a weight that was never yours.

Refusing to internalize a system's failure is an act of resistance. Naming the systemic context of your stress doesn't excuse you from the work of change — it gives you accurate information about what's actually going on, so you can direct your energy wisely.

Sort What's Personal vs Systemic

Which of your current challenges feel rooted in your habits, thoughts, or relationships? Which are shaped by external things — work demands, housing, policies, discrimination, access?

Are there areas where both overlap?

When people internalize systemic problems as personal failures, they direct their energy at changing themselves instead of addressing what's actually driving the distress. This is especially common among people experiencing poverty, racism, housing instability, or chronic health conditions — stressors that are real and structural, not personal deficiencies. Making the distinction doesn't remove responsibility where it genuinely exists. It ensures the work you do is aimed at the right target.

When the System Is the Problem — Your Role Looks Different

What boundary needs to be set? Who can support you? What action protects your values and your wellbeing without requiring you to fix something that isn't yours to fix?

In clinical practice, helping someone "cope better" with a genuinely unjust or harmful situation can inadvertently reinforce the idea that the problem is theirs to manage. When the stressor is structural — discrimination, poverty, a dehumanizing system — the right clinical move is sometimes naming that the system has failed, setting a boundary against internalising it, and identifying what action protects the person's values and wellbeing. Refusing to carry what isn't yours is an act of self-respect, not passivity.

You don't have to fix the system to protect yourself from it.

Even a small shift counts.

Did this help even a little?

You named something real — the load was never entirely yours.

Not everything hard is yours to fix. That's worth holding.

If this helped, you don't have to hold it alone.

That's okay. Stay with me. Try this instead.

Systemic stress can be overwhelming. Support is available.

Too much → Going deeper

Let's look at what's underneath the overwhelm.

These prompts are for when you have a little more space — not when you're flooded.

Step 1 — Name What's Really Going On

State it simply, without judgment. Not the whole story — just the core of it.

Write it badly if you need to. Write it just for you. Clarity comes later.

Step 2 — What's Yours to Carry?

Not everything in the fog is yours. Which parts feel personal — rooted in your habits or choices? Which are coming from outside?

Step 3 — What Would Help Most Right Now?

Not a solution. Just the next right direction. One small thing — internal or external — that would make this even slightly more manageable.

You looked at what's in the fog instead of just running from it.

You don't have to figure anything else out right now.

Emotions → Going deeper

Emotions are signals, not problems. Let's look at what yours might be saying.

What's the Emotion Underneath?

What you feel on the surface is often a secondary emotion — anger, irritability, numbness. Underneath is usually a primary feeling: hurt, fear, shame, grief.

Ask: "If I weren't angry right now, what would I be feeling?" That's usually where the real need lives.

Emotion → Need → Expression

Every emotion points to a need. Fill in what fits for you right now:

I feel
I need
I can express it by

Naming the need — not just the feeling — is one of the most powerful skills you can build.

Most emotional communication stops at the feeling — "I'm angry," "I'm hurt" — which leaves others with no clear path to respond. Naming the underlying need gives the other person something they can actually act on. It also changes the communication from an accusation to information. When you say "I need support" instead of "you never help," you're harder to argue with and easier to respond to. In recovery and mental health, many people have spent years suppressing or hiding needs entirely — learning to name them directly is a genuine skill that takes practice.

Rewrite the Myth

Which of these feel familiar?

  • "There's a right way to feel in every situation." → Emotions are responses, not grades.
  • "Being emotional means being out of control." → Feeling deeply and responding wisely are not opposites.
  • "Painful emotions should be ignored." → Ignored emotions don't disappear. They build pressure.

You stayed with something difficult long enough to understand it better.

You don't have to figure anything else out right now.

Thinking patterns → Going deeper

Let's look at one thought more carefully.

These prompts are for when you have a little more space — not when you're flooded.

The Thought Distortion Decoder

Pick the pattern that sounds most like what showed up for you:

All-or-nothing thinking

"If I mess this up, I've failed completely."
Trying to: Avoid gray areas. Try instead: "It's allowed to be imperfect and still count."

Catastrophizing

"This is going to ruin everything."
Trying to: Prepare for disaster. Try instead: "Hard? Yes. End of the world? Not quite."

Should statements

"I should be over this by now."
Trying to: Maintain control. Try instead: "It's taking the time it takes."

Labelling

"I'm just a failure."
Trying to: Simplify pain. Try instead: "I'm struggling with something. That doesn't define me."

Mind reading

"They think I'm annoying."
Trying to: Avoid rejection by predicting it. Try instead: "I can't know that — unless I ask."

Emotional reasoning

"I feel like a failure, so I must be."
Trying to: Trust internal signals. Try instead: "Feeling it doesn't make it true."

Personalization

"This is my fault."
Trying to: Maintain a sense of control. Try instead: "What actually caused this — and how much of it was mine?"

Filtering

"Everything went wrong."
Trying to: Protect against future disappointment. Try instead: "What actually went wrong — and what didn't?"

Fortune telling

"I know it's going to go badly."
Trying to: Prepare for failure before it happens. Try instead: "That's a prediction, not a fact. What's the actual evidence?"

Overgeneralising

"Nothing ever works out for me."
Trying to: Make sense of repeated pain. Try instead: "This happened. That doesn't mean it always happens."

Magnifying / minimising

"That mistake ruined everything" / "The good parts don't really count."
Trying to: Protect against being caught off guard. Try instead: "What's the actual size of this, honestly?"

Talk Back to the Thought

You don't have to believe the reframe yet. Just notice the gap between the thought and the more flexible version.

You sat with a thought instead of just being pulled by it.

You don't have to figure anything else out right now.

Values → Going deeper

Living a value and performing one are different things.

This is for when you have space to look more closely.

Am I Living This — or Just Performing It?

For each value that matters to you, ask yourself which column feels more honest right now:

Living it on purpose

Name one value you want to move into "living it on purpose":

Living it by accident
Not living it yet

This is a map, not a moral scorecard. No wrong answers.

Prompts to Go Deeper

  • What value do I act on — even when no one sees it?
  • What value do I keep betraying, even though I hate how that feels?
  • What have I stopped claiming because I didn't feel worthy of it?
  • If I could only carry three values right now — what would they be?

Values Are Direction, Not Destination

You don't have to embody them fully. You just have to start noticing them. What's one small move today that leans toward a value you want to live more fully?

You know what matters. That's the compass.

You don't have to figure anything else out right now.

Inner voices → Going deeper

Let's get to know this voice more fully.

Naming the noise is how you stop letting it steer.

Step 1 — When this voice shows up, how does it feel?

Step 2 — What does it say? Pick the sentence that fits most — then complete it.

Step 3 — What is it trying to do?

Even if it's been causing harm — it probably has a job.

Sometimes the job is outdated. You get to decide if it's still useful.

Step 4 — Give it a name.

The Inner Critic. The Alarm Bell. The Fence-Sitter. The Backseat Driver from Hell. Your character might be a cartoon possum with a clipboard. No wrong answers.

Step 5 — How do you want to respond to it?

What would you say back? What limits would you set? Can you thank it for trying — without letting it steer?

You just shifted from inside the voice to in front of it. From here, you get to decide whether it steers — or you do.

You looked at the critic directly and asked where it came from.

You don't have to figure anything else out right now.

Identity → Going deeper

Different versions of you have taken the lead at different times.

Each one got you through something. This is about recognizing them.

The Versions of You

Monarch butterflies don't make the journey from Canada to Mexico in one go. It takes four generations. One starts the flight. The next keeps going. Each one carries the instinct — even if they've never seen the destination.

We're like that too. The version that learned how to cope. The one that hid to stay safe. The one that dared to hope again. Each part got us through something.

Name the Versions

  • Which version of you showed up most in this season of your life?
  • Which version are you becoming?
  • What did the version that was just surviving need — that you can now offer yourself?
  • What would you want to say to the version of you that was hardest on yourself?

Reconstruction Prompts

You are not starting over. You are adding to your story with more intention.

The version of you that showed up today already knows something.

You don't have to figure anything else out right now.

Bigger picture → Going deeper

Some of what you're carrying has a source outside of you.

Naming it changes how you hold it.

What Are You Carrying That Might Not Belong to You Alone?

Check what fits — or write your own:

Generational pressure Racial injustice Gendered expectations Poverty or class shame Ableism Queer/transphobia Burnout from unpaid care Medical trauma

Challenge the Inherited Message

  • What's the message you've been taught to believe about this pain?
  • Are you internalizing a problem that's actually structural?
  • Who benefits when you believe the issue is just your fault?
  • If the system were built for care and fairness — what would be different?

Reclaim the Truth You Want to Tell

Rewriting doesn't have to be neat. It just has to be yours. What's one thing you want to say back to the narrative you've been handed?

A single word. A protest. A line of resistance. Whatever is honest.

Systems gain power when we internalize them. When a dominant narrative — "you're not trying hard enough," "this is just how it is" — gets absorbed as personal truth, the pain stays directed inward. Naming the source and speaking back to it doesn't erase the structural reality. But it interrupts the process by which a system's failure becomes a person's shame. That interruption is clinically significant.

That line — whatever you wrote — is yours. Systems lose power when we stop repeating what they told us about ourselves.

Not everything hard is yours to fix. You just proved you know the difference.

You don't have to figure anything else out right now.

A note to future you.

From the version of you that showed up today.

What you worked on today

You wrote this before

That version of you was also trying.

What I noticed — optional

Write to the version of you that might need this light again. Maybe it's you in a week. Maybe it's you months from now. You don't have to sound wise. Just be honest.

Your letter is never saved anywhere — it exists only while this tab is open. Usage patterns (what helped) are stored on your device only and never sent anywhere.

"This wasn't meant to change your life. It was meant to meet you where you were — to name what's going on, find a bit of direction, and maybe catch your breath."

Too much → Ride the urge

Hold still for a moment.

Don't act yet.

This feeling will peak and fall on its own. Your job is just to watch it.

What are you feeling pulled toward?

Just name it for yourself. You don't have to explain it.

Right now, how strong is the urge?

0 = barely there  ·  10 = overwhelming

5

How strong is it now?

5

Need to stop?

Okay. That happened.

It doesn't undo anything you did here.

Next time the wave starts, you'll have a bit more data about how it moves.

This is beyond what a tool should hold.

Please reach out to someone right now.

Too much → Urges

Hold still for a moment.

Don't act yet. Notice where you feel this in your body.

This feeling will peak and pass. You just need to outlast it.

Start here:

Ride It Out

This feeling rises, peaks, and falls on its own — usually within 10–20 minutes if you don't act on it. You don't have to make it disappear. You just have to stay with it a little longer.

  • Name what you feel pulled toward — just to yourself, not out loud
  • Notice where it sits in your body — heat, tension, restlessness
  • Breathe out slowly. Keep noticing. Don't engage with it.
Every time you stay with a strong urge without acting on it, you're demonstrating to yourself that it's survivable. The urge itself doesn't get weaker immediately — but your relationship to it changes. You're no longer someone who acts automatically. You're someone who can wait. That's not small.

Guided

Ride the wave — track the urge in real time as it peaks and falls.

Or try something else:

Check the Basics First

Strong urges often attach to unmet needs. Before anything else: are you hungry, angry, lonely, or tired? Address the underlying need and the pull often softens.

Urges don't appear from nowhere — they attach to states the body is already in. Hunger, exhaustion, isolation, and accumulated stress all lower your threshold for acting on impulse. Addressing the actual unmet need is often more effective than trying to fight the urge directly.

Buy Yourself 10 Minutes

You don't have to decide never. You just have to wait 10 minutes. Do something physical — walk, drink cold water, change rooms. Not to fix the feeling. Just to create space between the urge and the action.

If the urge is still there in 10 minutes, you can decide then. Most of the time, the intensity drops.

"You don't have to act on everything you feel."

Even a small shift counts.

Did this help even a little?

You stayed with it instead of acting on it.

That space between the feeling and the action — that's yours.

If this helped, you don't have to hold it alone.

That's okay. Stay with me. Try this instead.

If this feeling is becoming unsafe, please reach out now.

All tools

Search by name or browse by category.

No tools found.

About Maps and Mirrors

What This Is

Maps and Mirrors is a self-help support tool built on evidence-based approaches including CBT, ACT, DBT, and Narrative Therapy. It's designed to help you find direction and reflect on what matters — at your own pace, in your own way.

It works best alongside care — not instead of it.

A space for yourself

If you want to leave a note for the version of you that might need this light again, there's a space for that. You can find it at the bottom of any deeper reflection, or go there directly.

Privacy

This tool collects no personal information. There are no accounts, no login, and nothing is sent anywhere.

Your reflections — anything you write, name, or work through — stay on this device unless you copy or share them. They don't disappear when you close the tab, and nothing is ever transmitted.

Save to your phone

Tap ⋮ Menu → Add to Home Screen to save this tool to your phone. It works offline once installed.

Tap the Share button → Add to Home Screen to save this tool to your phone. It works offline once saved.

Open this page on your phone to save it to your home screen for quick access.

Share your feedback

If you have a moment, I'd love to hear about your experience with this tool. Your feedback helps improve it for everyone who uses it.

Share feedback →

Anonymous. Takes about 2 minutes.

If you need immediate support: Emergency: call 911 | Crisis: call or text 988

You've tried a few things. It might help to talk to someone.
